filtering-left-joins-with-on-and-where.label: Filtering LEFT JOINs with ON and WHERE
filtering-left-joins-with-on-and-where.summary: Place right-table conditions according to whether unmatched left rows must survive.

sketch1.title: Move One Condition and Watch the Result Change

try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.title: Preserve Every Department While Limiting Matches
try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.prompt: Return every department, but match only courses whose title contains `History`. Return `d.name AS department_name, c.title AS history_course` from `departments` in that column order. Use a LEFT JOIN to connect `courses` on `d.id = c.department_id AND c.title LIKE '%History%'`. Sort the final result by `d.name`.
try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.hint: Add the title condition after the key comparison inside the `ON` clause.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Use a LEFT JOIN to connect `courses` on `d.id = c.department_id AND c.title LIKE '%History%'`. Sort the final result by `d.name`.

try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.starterCode: -- Keep every department while matching only History courses
try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name AS department_name,
       c.title AS history_course
FROM departments AS d
LEFT JOIN courses AS c
  ON d.id = c.department_id
 AND c.title LIKE '%History%'
ORDER BY d.name;

try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.title: Move the Same Condition to WHERE
try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.prompt: Return `d.name AS department_name, c.title AS history_course` from `departments` in that column order. Use a LEFT JOIN to connect `courses` on `d.id = c.department_id`. Keep only rows where `c.title LIKE '%History%'`. Sort the final result by `d.name`.
try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.hint: Keep the key comparison in `ON`; move only the title condition below the join.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Keep only rows where `c.title LIKE '%History%'`. Sort the final result by `d.name`.

try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.starterCode: -- Move the same right-table filter to WHERE
try_filtering_left_joins_with_on_and_whe.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name AS department_name,
       c.title AS history_course
FROM departments AS d
LEFT JOIN courses AS c
  ON d.id = c.department_id
WHERE c.title LIKE '%History%'
ORDER BY d.name;

multi-choice-1.title: What Does LEFT JOIN Preserve?
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which statement correctly describes the preserved side of a LEFT JOIN?
multi-choice-1.hint: Decide whether unmatched left rows must survive.

help.concept: A right-table condition in `ON` limits matches; the same condition in `WHERE` can remove unmatched left rows.
help.hint_1: Decide whether the report must preserve left rows with no qualifying match.
help.hint_2: Compare the row count when the same right-table condition moves from `ON` to `WHERE`.

options.a: LEFT JOIN returns only matched rows from both tables.
options.b: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matched rows from the right table.
options.c: LEFT JOIN excludes unmatched rows from the left table.
options.d: LEFT JOIN is the same as INNER JOIN.

multi-choice-2.title: What Can a WHERE Filter Remove?
multi-choice-2.prompt: What happens when `WHERE` requires a value from the nullable right side of a LEFT JOIN?
multi-choice-2.hint: Think about how WHERE affects unmatched rows in a LEFT JOIN.

help.concept: Placing a condition in WHERE can filter out unmatched rows from the left table.
help.hint_1: WHERE filters the entire result set, potentially excluding unmatched rows.
help.hint_2: A WHERE condition can remove rows where the right table columns are NULL.

options.a: It filters the entire result set, potentially excluding unmatched rows.
options.b: It only affects the matched rows from the right table.
options.c: It has no effect on the result set.
options.d: It ensures all unmatched rows are included.

drag-reorder-1.title: Choose Where the Right-Table Filter Belongs
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the reasoning steps for deciding whether a right-table condition belongs in `ON` or `WHERE`.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Decide first whether unmatched left rows must remain.

help.concept: Filter placement follows the report requirement, not a memorized clause order.
help.hint_1: Start by identifying the preserved table and the rows the report must keep.
help.hint_2: Use `ON` to limit matches; use `WHERE` to filter the finished result.

tokens.t1: Identify which table's rows must be preserved
tokens.t2: Decide whether unmatched left rows must remain
tokens.t3: Put a match-limiting right-table condition in ON
tokens.t4: Put a final-result filter in WHERE

fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: In a LEFT JOIN, the condition that specifies how the tables are related is placed in the [blank1] clause.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Effect of WHERE Clause
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: Placing a condition in the WHERE clause of a LEFT JOIN can unintentionally remove [blank1] rows.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Consider what happens to the blank rows when a WHERE condition is applied.

help.concept: A WHERE condition can filter out the blank rows from the left table.
help.hint_1: WHERE conditions can exclude rows where the right table columns are NULL.
help.hint_2: the blank rows from the left table can be removed by a WHERE condition.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of LEFT JOIN
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of using a LEFT JOIN in SQL?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about what LEFT JOIN achieves compared to other types of joins.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN is used to include all rows from the left table, regardless of matches in the right table.
help.hint_1: LEFT JOIN ensures all rows from the left table are included.
help.hint_2: LEFT JOIN is useful for preserving unmatched rows from the left table.

options.a: To include only matched rows from both tables.
options.b: To include all rows from the left table and matched rows from the right table.
options.c: To exclude unmatched rows from the left table.
options.d: To perform a full outer join.

sketch0.title: ON chooses matches; WHERE chooses final rows
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A left join and a filter can appear in the same query while acting at different moments. The `ON` clause decides which right-side rows are allowed to match each left-side row. The `WHERE` clause runs after the join and decides which completed result rows remain.

Begin with every student and any enrollment that belongs to that student:

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.course_id
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```

A student without an enrollment survives with a `NULL` course id. Now add a final filter:

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.course_id
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
WHERE enrollments.course_id IS NOT NULL;
```

The join still creates the unmatched student row, but `WHERE` removes it because the condition is not true for `NULL`. The query now behaves like a report of enrolled students only.

This is why filter placement is part of the business question. Put a condition in `ON` when it should limit possible matches while preserving the left rows. Put it in `WHERE` when rows that fail the condition should disappear from the final result.

sketch1.title: The same condition can answer two different questions
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Compare two reports that both look for a course title containing `Writing`. The only change is where the condition appears.

In the first query, the condition belongs to the match rule:

```sql
SELECT departments.name, courses.title
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses
  ON departments.id = courses.department_id
 AND courses.title LIKE '%Writing%';
```

Every department remains. A department with a matching course shows its title; every other department shows `NULL`. The question is: **for each department, which Writing course matches, if any?**

In the second query, the condition filters the completed rows:

```sql
SELECT departments.name, courses.title
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses
  ON departments.id = courses.department_id
WHERE courses.title LIKE '%Writing%';
```

Departments without a matching title disappear because `NULL LIKE '%Writing%'` is not true. The question has changed to: **which joined rows contain a Writing course?**

Neither placement is universally better. The correct choice depends on whether unmatched left-side entities belong in the report. Predict that requirement first, then place the condition where it expresses the intended question.
